patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v3] btrfs: release extent lock per folio in readahead

version_history.subject: [PATCH v2] btrfs: release extent lock per folio in readahead
[PATCH v3] btrfs: release extent lock per folio in readahead

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch modifies the Btrfs file system's readahead behavior to release extent locks per folio, rather than holding them for the entire range. This change is intended to prevent a race condition between readahead and direct IO writes, which can cause tasks to hang on the extent lock. The problem arises when readahead fails due to memory allocation issues, leaving pages in an inconsistent state and causing subsequent reads to block on the extent lock. By releasing locks per folio, the patch aims to alleviate this issue and reduce stress on the system.

review_comments.author: Filipe Manana
review_comments.summary: Gave Reviewed-by

review_comments.author: Qu Wenruo

Thanks,

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH v2] btrfs: release extent lock per folio in readahead
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch addresses a contention issue on the extent lock in Btrfs, which occurs when multiple tasks are reading an overlapping range of a compressed file and one task is stuck in direct reclaim. The patch releases the extent lock per folio in readahead to alleviate some stress and prevent one thread's slowdown from escalating to others. This change is not critical but can help improve system health on large, loaded systems with high memory pressure.

review_comments.author: Filipe Manana
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer questioned whether the patch addresses a functional or performance issue, requesting clarification in the changelog.

Reviewer Filipe Manana noted that the patch does not address a potential deadlock scenario where two tasks reading an overlapping range of a compressed file can block each other due to contention on the extent lock, and requested further investigation.
review_comments.raw_body:
Than please add that information to the change log.
It's unclear whether this patch fixes a functional or a performance problem.

---

No need for data/numbers on that.

Thanks.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v4 0/9] dax/kmem: atomic whole-device hotplug via sysfs

version_history.subject: [PATCH v4 0/9] dax/kmem: atomic whole-device hotplug via sysfs

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 1/9] mm/memory: add memory_block_aligned_range() helper
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new helper function, memory_block_aligned_range(), which aligns a physical address range to memory block boundaries. This is necessary for memory hotplug operations and is used in the dax/kmem module.

review_comments.author: David Hildenbrand (Arm)
review_comments.summary: Acknowledged the patch without any issues or concerns.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 2/9] mm/memory_hotplug: pass online_type to online_memory_block() via arg
series_items.patch_summary: This patch modifies the `online_memory_block` function to accept an explicit online type through its argument, rather than relying on a default value. This change prepares for future scenarios where callers can specify custom online types. The patch also updates the `add_memory_resource` function to pass the default online type as a local variable. No functional changes are introduced by this patch.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 3/9] mm/memory_hotplug: export mhp_get_default_online_type
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series exports the `mhp_get_default_online_type` function to allow drivers that use DAX and hotplug policies to build when hotplug is disabled. The function returns `MMOP_OFFLINE` as a default, which is non-destructive.

review_comments.author: David Hildenbrand (Arm)
review_comments.summary: Acknowledged the patch and noted that it addresses a specific use case without CONFIG_MEMORY_HOTPLUG. Provided an ACK for the patch.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 4/9] mm/memory_hotplug: add __add_memory_driver_managed() with online_type arg
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new function, __add_memory_driver_managed(), which allows drivers to select the preferred online type (ZONE_NORMAL or ZONE_MOVABLE) when adding memory. The existing add_memory_driver_managed() function is refactored to use this new helper, and proper kernel documentation is added. This change is primarily for the CXL driver, but it's designed to be safe for in-tree modules that need to override the system-wide onlining policy.

review_comments.author: David (Arm)
review_comments.summary: Reviewer David (Arm) pointed out inconsistent indentation in the patch, specifically requesting two-tab indent on second parameter lines for new code and existing instances.
review_comments.raw_body:
We prefer two-tab indent on second parameter line while touching code / adding
new code.

Same applies to the other instances below.


Apart from that (still) LGTM.

-- 
Cheers,

David

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 5/9] mm/memory_hotplug: add multi-range hotunplug
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds the ability to offline and remove multiple contiguous memory ranges as one operation, ensuring atomicity and preventing race conditions that could leave some ranges in an inconsistent state. The new function, offline_and_remove_memory_ranges(), takes an array of ranges and processes them under a single lock, providing all-or-nothing semantics for the offline step. This is particularly useful for devices composed of multiple ranges, such as DAX or kmem, which can now call this function instead of looping over individual ranges. The patch addresses a problem where another daemon could online a block that was being offlined, causing the unplug operation to fail.

review_comments.author: David (Arm)
review_comments.summary: Reviewer David suggested generalizing the logic in offline_and_remove_memory() to handle multiple ranges, and proposed making offline_and_remove_memory() a wrapper around offline_and_remove_memory_ranges()
review_comments.raw_body:
Is there a way to just generalize the logic in offline_and_remove_memory() to
multiple ranges, making offline_and_remove_memory() then a simple wrapper around
the new offline_and_remove_memory_ranges(), providing only a single range?

-- 
Cheers,

David

review_comments.author: Gregory Price (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledges a need to address the issue of callers managing multiple ranges and promises to investigate further.
sentiment_signals: promised to look at what can be done
review_comments.raw_body:
Yeah that's reasonable, I'll look at what can be done.

~Gregory

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 6/9] dax: plumb hotplug online_type through dax
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds the ability for drivers using dax_kmem to specify an auto-online policy when creating DAX devices. It does this by adding an 'online_type' field to the dev_dax_data structure, which can be set to a specific memory hotplug online type. This allows drivers to choose their preferred auto-online policy instead of relying on the system default. The patch makes no functional changes to existing callers and is intended to provide more flexibility for DAX device creation.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 7/9] dax/kmem: extract hotplug/hotremove helper functions
series_items.patch_summary: This patch refactors the kmem probe and remove functions in the dax/kmem driver by extracting initialization, cleanup, hotplug, and hot-remove logic into separate helper functions. The helpers enable future extensions to support more granular control over memory hotplug operations. The patch also addresses an issue where resources are not registered when probing occurs, preventing dax devices from being added to sysfs.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 8/9] dax/kmem: add sysfs interface for atomic hotplug
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a sysfs interface to control and query the state of an entire region of memory managed by the dax kmem driver. The new 'hotplug' attribute allows userspace to set the state of the memory region, which can be either online (in one of three possible zones) or unplugged. The patch also introduces a new 'unplugged' state that only applies to kmem/hotplug and provides atomic hot-unplug functionality across the entire device. This addresses a race condition in the previous implementation where external entities could operate on memory blocks while they were being removed, leading to failed hot-unplug operations.

review_comments.author: Gregory Price (author)
review_comments.summary: The author is addressing a concern about adding MMOP_UNPLUGGED as a valid state for memory hotplug, which would allow users to permanently unplug memory without the ability to get it back. The author agrees to add this state but also notes that additional code changes are needed to disallow MMOP_UNPLUGGED as a valid input.
review_comments.raw_body:
In patch 6 response i point out adding MMOP_UNPLUGGED

If we add MMOP_UNPLUGGED as a state that is only use by callers of
memory hotplug to represent the current state - but not as a valid input
to memory_hotplug.c - then we can simply this as you request.

Although we'll need to add a couple lines to memoryN/state parsing code
to disallow MMOP_UNPLUGGED as a valid input (otherwise you could
permanently unplug memory without the ability to get it back... unless
you want that?  Seems useless to me.)

~Gregory

review_comments.author: David (Arm)
review_comments.summary: Reviewer David suggested special-casing the 'unplugged' state in dax_kmem_parse_state() and using the existing core helpers, eliminating the need to add a new value to MM core.

review_comments.author: Gregory Price (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledges reviewer's suggestion to use MMOP_UNPLUGGED, agrees to implement it in a future patch (not this one), and thanks the reviewer for their input.
sentiment_signals: agrees to implement in a future patch
review_comments.raw_body:
Was going to propose this, but then I thought... well if CXL detactes
itself from dax and does this same pattern, maybe MMOP_UNPLUGGED makes
sense.

But i'll leave that for another day and do as you suggest.

Thanks!

~Gregory

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 9/9] selftests/dax: add dax/kmem hotplug sysfs regression test
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a selftest to verify the functionality of the dax/kmem hotplug sysfs attribute, which allows transitioning a kmem-backed dax device between 'unplugged', 'online' and 'online_movable' states. The test discovers an existing kmem-bound dax device, skips if none is found or memory cannot be freed, and then validates the interface contract by checking for correct behavior in various scenarios, including online -> unplug -> online_movable -> unplug cycle.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series adds atomic whole-device hotplug control via sysfs, allowing for transitions to be atomic across entire device ranges. The patch addresses a race condition between offlining and removing memory blocks, which was discussed in the LPC2025 device memory sessions. It introduces a 'hotplug' attribute and updates mm and dax plumbing to support it. The state names mirror the per-block memoryX/state ABI with modifications for atomic whole-device control. The patch also includes mm preparation changes, such as adding helpers for online type and offline+remove of multiple ranges under a single lock.

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [Lsf-pc] [LSF/MM/BPF TOPIC][RFC PATCH v4 00/27] Private Memory Nodes (w/ Compressed RAM)
Re: [Lsf-pc] [LSF/MM/BPF TOPIC][RFC PATCH v4 00/27] Private Memory Nodes (w/ Compressed RAM)
Re: [PATCH v2] cgroup/cpuset: rebind mm mempolicy to effective_mems, not mems_allowed
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: The patch author is asked to revert a change made in v2, switching back to 'newmems' from 'effective_mems', as it introduces dependency on another code path.

review_comments.author: Gregory Price
review_comments.summary: Identified a potential issue with dependency on another code path and requested a change back to 'newmems'.

review_comments.author: David Hildenbrand (Arm)
review_comments.summary: Acknowledged the request to revert the change, citing Waiman's original request and expressing agreement with Gregory's assessment.

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH v2] cgroup/cpuset: rebind mm mempolicy to effective_mems, not mems_allowed
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch updates how memory policies are bound to CPU sets in the Linux kernel. It changes the binding from `mems_allowed` to `effective_mems`, which is necessary because `mems_allowed` is protected by a task lock and cannot be accessed for foreign tasks. This fix resolves inconsistencies and potential SIGBUS errors that can occur when interacting with memory policies and CPU sets.

review_comments.author: David (Arm)
review_comments.summary: Reviewer David (Arm) expressed concern that the patch does not guarantee a non-empty mempolicy in all cgroup v1 scenarios, and suggested using newmems which is guaranteed to return something non-empty.
sentiment_signals: concern about obscure cgroup v1 scenario
review_comments.raw_body:
Well, we can just use newmems, which cannot change? Again, that is based on
cs->effective_mems but is guaranteed to return something non-empty.

AI was not able to convince me (neither was I able to convince AI) that there is
not some obscure cgroup v1 scenario where the current fix would also be wrong.

With newmems it's clear that it is guaranteed to not be empty.

-- 
Cheers,

David

review_comments.author: Gregory Price (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledged that reverting to 'newmems' is necessary because relying on a derivative value ('effective_mems') could lead to inconsistencies, and agreed to make the change.
review_comments.raw_body:
I hadn't noticed he switched the patch from newmems -> effective_mems.

This needs to be changed back to newmems, otherwise we're depending on
a derivative value set somewhere else in the code being correct instead
of using what we *know* is correct *at the moment we need to use it*.

So yes, go back to using newmems.

~Gregory

review_comments.author: David (Arm)
review_comments.summary: Reviewer David expressed disagreement with the patch's change, citing that it was reverted in v1 and questioning its necessity.
review_comments.raw_body:
Right, that's what v1 did looking at this now. Waiman requested the change, but
I don't think we want that.

So for v1:

Acked-by: David Hildenbrand (Arm) <david@kernel.org>

-- 
Cheers,

David

review_comments.author: Waiman Long
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Waiman Long noted that the patch's use of effective_mems instead of mems_allowed could lead to inconsistent behavior during the transition period when a memory node is being hotunplugged, and suggested reverting to using cs->effective_mems to maintain the old cgroup v1 behavior.
review_comments.raw_body:
That is what I believe why mpol_rebind_mm() a bit differently from the 
others and it is historically done this way a long time ago before 
cgroup v2.

For cgroup v1, mems_allowed can't be empty or you can't put any task 
into the cpuset. Also effective_mems is the same as mems_allowed. cgroup 
v2 is quite different in how it handles memory nodes and CPUs. Users can 
isn't forced to set mems_allowed and cpus_allowed as effective_mems and 
effective_cpus will inherit parent version if mems_allowed and 
cpus_allowed are not set. IOW, effective_mems will never be empty. Yes, 
it is a bug with the introduction of cpuset v2 that we should have 
replaced mems_allowed by effective_mems at that time. With v2, 
effective_mems should contain only online nodes. The only exception is 
during the short transition period when a memory node hotunplug 
operation is in progress when a write to cpuset.mems is happening at the 
same time. With v1, it is theoretically possible that none of the nodes 
in mems_allowed is online.

The reason why I am suggesting to use cs->effective_mems to keep the old 
cgroup v1 behavior. If the consensus is to use the output of 
guarantee_online_mems() for mpol_rebind_mm(), I will not be against that 
but it will be a slight change in user-visible behavior.

Cheers, Longman

review_comments.author: Waiman Long
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Waiman Long prefers the original v2 patch and suggests that if a change is made, it should be in a separate patch with updated documentation.
sentiment_signals: no clear technical objection or suggestion
review_comments.raw_body:
BTW, I still prefer the v2 patch. If it is decided we should use the 
guarantee_online_mems() value instead, it will have to be a separate 
patch with changes in the relevant documentation like 
Documentation/admin-guide/cgroup-v1/cpuset.rst.

Cheers,
Longman

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v5] nfsd: validate sockaddr length per family in listener_set

version_history.subject: [PATCH v3] nfsd: validate sockaddr length per family in listener_set
[PATCH v4] nfsd: validate sockaddr length per family in listener_set
[PATCH v5] nfsd: validate sockaddr length per family in listener_set

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch introduces a new helper function, nfsd_nl_validate_listeners(), to validate the listener list from userland before making any changes. The function checks each entry for well-formedness, including parsing against policy, carrying both an address and transport name, and ensuring the address length is sufficient for its family. This validation is done upfront to prevent malformed requests from causing side effects.

review_comments.author: Chuck Lever
review_comments.summary: Identified that the patch addresses a potential security issue and suggested applying it to the nfsd-testing branch. The reviewer was satisfied with the patch's changes and applied it.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Mon, 15 Jun 2026 14:31:25 -0400, Jeff Layton wrote:
> nfsd_sock_nl_policy declares NFSD_A_SOCK_ADDR as a bare NLA_BINARY
> attribute with no minimum length. A CAP_NET_ADMIN caller can send a
> 16-byte NFSD_A_SOCK_ADDR with sa_family=AF_INET6, causing a 12-byte
> OOB read across three consumers (rpc_cmp_addr_port, svc_find_listener,
> kernel_bind).
> 
> nfsd_nl_listener_set_doit() also parsed and validated each listener
> entry inline in two separate loops, interleaved with mutating the
> running listener configuration. The validation was duplicated, used an
> open-coded "nla_len < sizeof(struct sockaddr)" check that was too short
> for AF_INET6, and handled a malformed entry inconsistently depending on
> which loop noticed it.
> 
> [...]

Applied to nfsd-testing, thanks!

[1/1] nfsd: validate sockaddr length per family in listener_set
      commit: 1f102d9ff00620b845546051ad1ee57976f2db88

--
Chuck Lever
